text: CHATEAU-L'EVEQUE, or PREYSSAC D'AGONE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Dordogne, cant. of Périgueux. Pop. 1,590.

CHATEAULIN, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Finistère. The arrond. comprises an area of 181,257 hect.; and contains 7 cant., viz.: Carhaix, Chateaulin, Chateauneuf - du - Faou, Crozon, Le Faou, Huelgoat, and Pleyben. Pop. in 1831, 94,302; in 1841, 100,520; in 1846, 104,053. — The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 16,710; in 1841, 17,568.—The town, which is of considerable antiquity, is situated in a picturesque valley, 21 m. N of Quimper; and is intersected by the Aulne, which here takes the name of the town, and is crossed by a narrow bridge. Pop. in 1789, 2,314; in 1821, 2,514; in 1836, 2,968; and in 1841, 2,548. It has a castle, and a small port. The houses are ill and irregularly built. The trade, which is active, consists in cattle, fish, chiefly salmon from the river, fowls, grain, hemp, lint, butter, slate from the neighbouring quarries, iron, cloth, and earthenware. Fairs are held once a-month.
CHATEAU-MARGAUX, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. of Castelnau-de-Médoc, and com. of Margaux, 16 m. NW of Bordeaux.

CHATEAU-MEILLANT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Cher, arrond. of Saint-Amand-Mont-Rond. The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,287; in 1841, 10,430.— The town, the ancient Mediolanum, stands on the brow of a hill, 21 m. SW of Saint-Amand-Mont- Rond. Pop. in 1846, 2,777. It contains an ancient castle, and a tower said to have been built by Julius Cæsar. Fairs are held nine times a-year.

CHATEAUNEUF, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Côte-d'Or, cant. of Pouilly, 19 m. NW of Beaune. Pop. 460.—Also a commune and town in the dep. of the Saône-et-Loire, cant. of Chauffailles, 18 m. S of Charolles. Pop. 242.—Also a commune in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Manzat, on the l. bank of the Sioule, 18 m. from Riom. It is noted for its mineral springs and baths. Pop. in 1846, 935.—Also a hamlet in the dep. of the Vienne, cant. and com. of Châtellerault. Pop. in 1831, 3,500.—Also a canton, commune, and town in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, arrond. of Limoges. -The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,163; in 1841, 11,134.—The town is on the Combade, 21 m. from Limoges. Pop. in 1846, 1,345. Fairs are held eight times a-year.

CHATEAUNEUF-EN-BRETAGNE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, arrond. of St. Malo. The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,542; in 1841, 12,132. -The town is on the Auzon, 7 m. SE of St. Malo. Pop. in 1841, 756. It has a fort which stands on an isthmus, and which was constructed in 1777 to defend the coast. Fairs are held four times a-year. The trade consists chiefly in cattle, agricultural implements, earthen-ware, and common stuffs.

CHATEAUNEUF - CALCERNIER, or CHATEAUNEUF-DU-PAPE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. and 6 m. SW of Orange, near the l. bank of the Rhone. Pop. in 1846, 1,351. The environs are celebrated for their wine.

CHATEAUNEUF-SUR-CHARENTE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Charente, arrond. of Cognac. The cant. comprises 18 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,079; in 1841, 10,603.— The town is situated on the l. bank of the Charente, 19 m. ESE of Cognac. Pop. in 1846, 2,739. It has an active trade in wine, brandy, essences, salt, staves, cattle, &c. Fairs are held monthly. This town was originally called Berdeville. It was formerly a place of some strength.

CHATEAUNEUF-SUR-CHER, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Cher, arrond. of Saint-Amand-Mont-Rond. — The canton comprises 12 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,510.—The com. and town are on an island in the Cher, 15 m. SSW of Bourges. It has some commerce in wine and cattle. Pop. 2,219 in 1841.

CHATEAUNEUF-DE-FAOU, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Finistere, arrond. of Chateaulin.—The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 16,030.—The com. and town, on the r. bank of the Aulne, 14 m. E of Chateaulin, had a pop. of 2,506 in 1841.
CHATEAUNEUF-DE-GADAGNE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. of L'Isle. Pop. 1,108.

CHATEAUNEUF-DE-GALAURE, a commune | Caddis and kalmouks are its only manufactures. ベストラスト ストラスト カスト ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ストライン・ス CHA and village of France, in the dep. of Drome, cant. of Saint-Vallier. Pop. 1,203.
CHATEAUNEUF - D'ISE'RE, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Drome, arrond. of Bourg-du-Peage, on the l. bank of the Isère. Pop. 2,264.

CHATEAUNEUF-SUR-LOIRE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Lorret, arrond. of Orleans.—The cant., comprising 12 com., had a pop. of 10,137 in 1831.—The com. and town, on the r. bank of the Loire, 15 m. E of Orleans, had a pop. of 2,994 in 1841. It has manufactories of woollens and serges; and some commerce in vinegar and honey.
CHATEAUNEUF - DE - MAZENE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Drome, cant. of Dieulefit, 10 m. E of Montelimart. Pop. 1,698. There are silk manufactories here.

CHATEAUNEUF - DE - RANDON, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Lozere, arrond. of Mende.—The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1831, 3,705.—The com. and v., 16 m. NE of Mende, had a pop. of 607 in 1841. This place was anciently fortified; and there is here a monument to Duguesclin, who fell, in 1380, while laying siege to the town, then occupied by the English.
CHATEAUNEUF - DU - RHONE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Drome, cant. of Montelimart, on the l. bank of the Rhone, opposite Viviers. Pop. 1,333.
CHATEAUNEUF-LE-ROUGE, or NEGREL, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Bouches-du-Rhone, cant. of Trets. Pop. 401.

CHATEAUNEUF - SUR - SARTHE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Maineet-Loire, arrond. of Segré. - The cant., comprising 15 com., had a pop. of 12,678 in 1831.—The com. and town, on the r. bank of the Sarthe, 18 m. E of Segré, had a pop. of 1,391 in 1841.

CHATEAUNEUF - EN - THYMERAIS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, arrond. of Dreux. - The cant. comprises 29 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,183.—The com. and town, 14 m. SSW of Dreux, had a pop. of 1,231 in 1841.
CHATEAUNEUF-VAL-DE-BARGIS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Nièvre, com. of Donzy, 18 m. SE of Cosne. Pop. 2,057.

CHATEAU-PONSAT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, arrond. of Bellac. The cant., comprising 6 com., had a pop. of 9,308 in 1831.—The com., 10 m. E of Bellac, on the r. bank of the Gartempe, had a pop. in 1841 of 3,837.

CHATEAU-PORCIEN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Ardennes, arrond. of Réthel. The cant. comprises 16 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,494; in 1841, 9,926. The town is situated partly on the r. bank and on an island of the Aisne, 6 m. W of Rethel, and 29 m. SW of Mizières. Pop. in 1841, 2,463. It is commanded by an ancient fortress, which stands on the summit of a steep rock. Woollen fabrics and leather form the chief articles of local manufacture and commerce.

CHATEAU-RENARD, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Bouches-du- Rhone, arrond. of Arles. The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,573; in 1841, 14,418. The town is near the l. bank of the Durance, 13 m. NE of Arles, at the foot of a hill on which are the ruins of an ancient castle. Pop. in 1841, 4,744. Its principal streets are well-paved; and it contains a fine promenade, a public fountain, and a town-house. The environs afford good wine. - Also a canton, commune, and town in the dep. of the Loiret, arrond. of Montargis. The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,352; in 1841, 11,143. The town is on the Ouanne, 10 m. ESE of Montargis. It has important manufactories of cloth and serge; and possesses some trade in wool, saffron, and timber.
CHATEAU-RENAUD, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saône-et-Loire, cant. of Louhans. Pop. 1,382.

CHATEAU - RENAULT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Indre-et- Loire, arrond. of Tours. The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,658; in 1841, 11,823.—The town is on the Brenne, 19 m. NNE of Tours, on the road from Paris to Bordeaux. Pop. in 1841, 2,877. It contains several manufactories of common cloth and hosiery, extensive tanneries and tile-works, and some fulling and tanning-mills; and has a considerable trade in timber, grain, and clover-seed.

CHATEAUROUX, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Indre.—The arrond. comprises an area of 247,375 hect.; and contains 8 cant., viz.: Ardentes-Saint- Vincent, Argenton-sur-Creuse, Buzançois, C., Chatillon-sur-Indre, Ecueillé, Levroux, and Valencay. Pop. in 1831, 91,289; in 1846, 98,743.—The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 19,606.—The town is situated in an extensive plain, on the L bank of the Indre, in N lat. 46° 48′ 45″, E long. 1° 42′, on the road from Paris to Limoges, 157 m. SSW of Paris. It is the seat of a communal college and a normal school. Its principal manufacture is that of woollens; and it forms an entrepot for the wine-trade of the district. Pop. in 1836, 13,847; in 1846, 13,551. Under the old republic it was known as Indreville.— Also a commune of France, in the dep. of the Hautes-Alpes, cant. and 6 m. N of Embrun. Pop. in 1841, 1,747.

CHATEAU-SALINS, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Meurthe. -The arrond. has an area of 108,047 hectares, and comprises the 5 cant. of Albestroff, C., Dolme, Dieuzè, and Vic. Pop. in 1841, 70,326.—The cant. comprises 38 com. Pop. in 1831, 15,230.—The com. and town are 18 m. NE of Nancy, on the r. bank of the Petite Seille. Pop. in 1821, 2,851; in 1841, 2,468. Its chief articles of manufacture are cloths, glass, and leather. The town owes its name to a salt-spring which has been employed in the manufacture of salt since the year 1330, and still produces 220,000 quintals of salt annually.

CHATEAU-THE BAUD, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire - Inferieure, cant. of Vertue. Pop. 1,682. CHATEAU-THIERRY,—under the old republic, EGALITE'-SUR-MARNE, -an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aisne. -The arrond, has an area of 115,526 hectares, and comprises the 5 cant. of C., Charly, Condé-en-Brie, Fere-en-Tardenois, and Neuilly-Saint-Front. Pop. in 1831, 60,771; in 1846, 64,448.—The cant. comprises 22 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,491.—The com. and town are situated on the Marne, 47 m. E by N of Paris. Pop. in 1789, 3,926; in 1846, 5,200. It carries on a considerable commerce in grain, wool, cattle, sheep, and wine; and has a very large annual sheep fair in May or June. Its chief manufactures are linens, cottons, and leather. The ruins of the ancient castle founded by Charles Martel in 720, and from which it takes its name, surmount a hill overlooking the town. -The poet La Fontaine was born here; and his statue adorns the bridge crossing the Marne. CHATEAU-VILLAIN, -under the old republic, VILLE-SUR-ANJOU, -a canton, commune, and town CHA of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, arrond. of Chaumont.—The cant. comprises 19 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,167.—The com. and town, on the Anjou, 12 m. SW of Chaumont, had a pop. of 2,068 in 1841.
CHATEAU-VILLE-VIEILLE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Alpes, cant. of Aiguilles. Pop. 1,378.
CHATEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, cant. of Grand-Pre. Pop. 720.
CHAT-EL-ARAB. See SHAT-EL-ARAB.

CHATELARD (LE), a village of Savoy, on the r. bank of the Cheran, 12 m. NE of Chambery. Pop. 1,000.—Also a commune of France, in the dep. of Basses-Alpes, cant. of Barcelonette. Pop. 586.— Also a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, near the lake of Geneva, at an alt. of 494 metres above sea-level. Pop. 2,066.

CHATELAUDREN, or CHATEAU-LAUDREN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, arrond. of Saint-Brieux, on the Leff. The cant. comprises 8 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,075. The com. is 9 m. W by N of Saint-Brieux Pop. 964.
CHATEL-CENSOIR, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Vezelay, on the r. bank of the Yonne. Pop. 1,207.

CHATELDON, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, arrond. of Thiers. The cant., comprising 6 com., had a pop. of 7,602 in 1831. The com., 10 m. N of Thiers, had a pop. of 1,733 in 1841.
CHATELEE, a village of Anatolia, 20 m. NNW of Isburta. Arundell suggests that the ancient Silbium was at or near this v.

CHATELET, a commune and town of Belgium, in the dep. of Hainault, arrond. and 4 m. E of Charleroi, on the r. bank of the Sambre. Pop. in 1836, 2,613. This is a place of active industry, and has considerable manufactories of woollens.

CHATELET (LE), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Cher, arrond. of Saint- Amand-Mont-Rond. The cant., comprising 8 com., had a pop. of 6,348 in 1831. The com., 12 m. SW of Saint-Amand-Mont-Rond, had a pop. of 1,368 in 1841.—Also a canton, com., and v. in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, arrond. of Melun. The cant., comprising 14 com., had a pop. of 8,456 in 1831. The com., 6 m. ESE of Melun, had a pop. of 1,106 in 1841.
CHATEL-GERARD, a commune of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Noyers. Pop. 644.
CHATELGUYON, or SAINT-COUST-CHATELGUYON, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Riom. Pop. 1,718. There are mineral springs and baths here.

CHATELLERAULT, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of La Vienne.—The arrond. has an area of 109,040 hectares; and comprises the 6 cant. of C., Dange, Leigné-sur-Usseau, L'Encloitre, Pleumartin, and Vouneuil-sur-Vienne.—The cant. comprises 7 com., and had a pop. of 14,905 in 1831.—The com. and town are 18 m. NNE of Poitiers, on the Vienne, which flows through it. It is a meanly built town, but is a place of active industry, and one of the chief seats of the cutlery manufacture in France. It is also a considerable entrepot in the river trade. Pop. in 1789, 7,200; in 1846, 11,298. The title of duke of C. was bestowed on an ancestor of the Scotch duke of Hamilton in 1548.
CHATEL-MONTAGNE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Mayet-de- Montagne, on the r. bank of the Bobre. Pop. 1,768.

CHATEL-SUR-MOSELLE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Vosges, arrond. of Epinal. The cant. comprises 24 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,067. The com., 10 m. NNW of Epinal, has a pop. of 1,176.
CHATEL-DE-NEUVRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Allier, cant. of Montel. Pop. 820.
CHATEL-SAINT-DENIS, or KASTELS, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Friburg, on the Vevayse, at an alt. of 2,660 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 2,275.

CHATELUS, or CHATELLUX, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Creuse. The cant., comprising 11 com., had a pop. of 11,008 in 1841. The com., 10 m. SSW of Boussac, has a pop. of 1,094 in 1841.
CHATELUS-DE-MARCHEIX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Creuse, cant. of Benevent, on the r. bank of the Thorion. Pop. 1,495.
CHATENAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loir, cant. of Auneau. Pop. 365.—Also a com. in the dep. of Seine, cant. and 1 m. SW of Sceaux. Pop. 699.

CHATENOIS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, cant. of Schelestat. Pop. 3,817. There are here manufactures of cotton and linen, and paper-works.—Also a commune and town in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, 6 m. S of Belfort. Pop. 1,128.—Also a cant. and com. in the dep. of Vosges. The cant., comprising 26 com., had a pop. of 11,589 in 1831. The com., 8 m. SE of Neufchateau, had a pop. of 1,593 in 1841.

CHATHAM, a parliamentary burgh, market-town, and parish, adjoining or forming a suburb to the city of Rochester, but chiefly in the hund. of C. and Gillingham, co. of Kent, 30 m. E by S of London, on the SE bank of the Medway, and the N side of C.-hill. With Rochester and Stroud, C. forms one continuous street 2 m. in length, but has been rendered much less of a thoroughfare by the new road to Canterbury being carried to the S of the town without entering it. The whole parish is extensive, running for 3 or 4 m. to the S; a small part of it is within the liberties of the city of Rochester. There is a row of houses a short distance out of the town on C.-hill; the rest of the p. is entirely agricultural, containing a small village at Lutton, and a few scattered houses. C. communicates with London by the Dover and Canterbury road; the distance by this road varying but little from the direct distance. But the most important channel of communication is the Medway, which falls into the estuary of the Thames at Sheerness, and floats the largest ships to C. Indeed, in consequence of works begun in the reign of Charles II., and at various periods subsequently resumed, the Medway has been rendered navigable as far as Tonbridge; and a canal was cut from the Thames opposite Tilbury fort to C., a distance of about 9 m., of which 2 m. were tunnelled. This canal has been converted into a branch line of the South-eastern railway, known as the Gravesend and Rochester. railway; which has recently been connected with the metropolis by the North Kent line. A line of railway between London and C., commencing by a junction with the London and Greenwich railway at Ravensbone river, or Deptford creek, and thence proceeding, through Woolwich, Dartford, Gravesend, and Chalk, to Frindsbury, where it is to terminate, in a marsh adjoining the Medway, opposite the town of C., with which it is to be connected by floating bridges or ferry-boats worked by steam, was projected a few years ago; and a direct railway from C. to Portsmouth, by a line branching off from the London and Brighton railroad at Riegate, was also among the schemes of recent years. The town is irregularly built, and presents in itself few buildings of any special interest. The streets are narrow, and the older houses mean and ill-built. The church is a plain modern structure of brick containing a num- CHA ber of elegant monuments. It stands on the chalk cliff rising above the Ordnance wharf. To accommodate the increasing population, a district church has been erected by the parliamentary commissioners, at an expense of £13,920. There is also an elegant chapel in the dock-yard; and the Independents, Baptists, Bible Christians, and Unitarians, have chapels here. There are two considerable charity hospitals in this parish, St. Bartholomew's hospital and Hawkins's hospital.—The area of the p. is returned, with that of Rochester city, at 6,150 acres. The pop. of the town, excluding Brampton and the barracks, was returned in 1841 at 13,468. The principal part of the borough is in the parish of C.; but a part extends into the p. of Gillingham on the east. The number of the pop. within the parliamentary boundary in 1841 was 17,903. The registered voters in 1837 were 777; in 1847, 1,159. By the Reform act, C. was united with Rochester in returning members to parliament; but it was afterwards separated, and now returns a member for itself. The town is partly in the jurisdiction of the county-magistrates, and partly in the jurisdiction of the town of Rochester. Horse-races are annually held at C. in the month of August; and fairs, of three days' continuance, each commencing on 15th May and 20th October. The inhabitants are chiefly employed in the dock-yard on the E side of the river, or in trades connected with maritime pursuits. General description of the Docks.] Till converted into one of the naval arsenals of England, C. was nothing more than what its name implies,—a paltry cluster of cottages anciently called Cettham, from the Saxon cyte, 'a cottage,' and ham, 'a village.' Its celebrity and consequence have arisen entirely from the suitableness of its position as the site of a national dockyard and naval arsenal. Situated on the banks of a river, which, considering the shortness of its course, is one of the deepest of European rivers, and in sufficient proximity to the metropolis, it has become the site of an extensive naval establishment including the finest naval arsenal in the world. These important works occupy an extensive area on the N side of the town, measuring nearly 1 m. in length, and defended, on the land-side, by strong fortifications principally of modern origin. The dock-yard, extending over an area of 90 acres, was commenced in the reign of Elizabeth. The original part of it is now the ordnance wharf, to which purpose it was appropriated by James I., who, finding it too small and inconvenient for the increasing business of the navy, had the present dock made somewhat further to the N. This was again enlarged and greatly improved by his son, Charles L, who erected capacious store-houses, and constructed docks to enable ships to float in with the tide. It was further improved by Charles II., in whose reign, in 1677, the Dutch, under De Ruyter and Van Ghent, after taking and destroying Sheerness, sailed up the Medway with 17 light-ships and 8 fire-ships to the very front of Upnor castle, destroying several sail of the line and a great quantity of stores. They accomplished this daring and brilliant achievement without incurring any material loss; but the fortifications were soon after so much improved and strengthened as to render a coup de main of this sort no longer possible. The dock-yard is surrounded by a high wall; the entrance is by a spacious gateway flanked by embattled towers. The houses of the principal officers are handsomely built, and the offices in the several departments of the yard neatly and commodiously arranged. The numerous and extensive store-houses contain immense quantities of every essential for naval equipment, so arranged that even a first-rate can be fully equipped in a few days. There are great mast-houses, and capacious basins with floating mast-timber. In the new rope-house, which is above 11,000 ft. long, enormous cables are twisted by powerful machinery. In the smith's shop, which contains 40 forges, every article necessary for ship-building, from the smallest bolt to an anchor of 5 tons in weight, is made. A newly-invented blastengine, consisting of fanners worked by steam-power, and so disposed as to send the air with great force through pipes from a distance to the smithery fires, was first tried here in June 1840; and was found to be so effective that one-half of the blast-which can be turned on like gas or water to any requisite degreeproduces welding heats, while the whole force blows the fire entirely out. A duplicate of Mr. Brunel's block-making machine is kept here ready for use, in case the machine at Portsmouth should get out of order. The saw-mills, erected under the superintendence of Mr. Brunel, are upon a most extensive scale. In the sawing-room, which is 90 ft. square, are fixed 8 saw-frames, each capable of carrying from 1 to 30 saws, and 2 circular saw benches, with windlasses and capstans for supplying them with wood, the whole being set in motion by a powerful steam-engine which produces 80 strokes of the saws per minute. Connected with this steam-engine are extensive water-works for the supply of the yard; and in the iron pipes laid down in various parts of the yard are fire-plugs, from which, when opened, the water is thrown far above the summits of the highest buildings. The wet-docks, four in number, are sufficiently capacious for first rate men-of-war, and there is a new stone dock constructed upon a still larger scale. In the docks are six slips or launches for building or repairing ships of the largest dimensions. Second and third rates have also been built here, besides frigates. The wood is floated into the yard, by a canal which passes through a tunnel 300 ft. long, into an elliptic basin of immense depth, whence it is raised by machinery.—On the ordnance wharf, which occupies a narrow neck of land between the chapel and the river, the guns belonging to each ship are laid up in tiers, with the weight of metal and the name of the ship to which they belong marked on them. The gun-carriages are laid up under cover, and immense quantities of shot are piled up pyramidally in various places. Every department has its proper officer, and nothing can exceed the order and despatch with which every thing is executed here. The number of artificers and labourers employed is at all times great, but, in time of war, it commonly exceeds 3,000. The defence of this important arsenal was originally confided principally to guard-ships stationed in the river, aided first by Upnor castle, and by fortifications thrown up at Sheerness, which were strengthened and extended at different periods, with the special purpose of adding security to C. As its stores were accumulated, however, its importance became more apparent; and, in 1758, an act was passed for purchasing ground and constructing such works as should place it in perfect security. Under this act the extensive fortifications called 'the Lines' were erected. These lines commence on the banks of the Medway, above the ordnance wharf, and continue from the banks of the river round an oblong plot of ground, measuring about a 1 m. in width, and 1 m. long, to beyond the extremity of the dockyard, where they again join with the river. Within this area, besides the naval establishments, are included the Upper and Lower barracks, the church of C., and the v. of Brompton, pleasantly situated on the summit of the high ground to the SE of the yard. These lines are enlarged and strengthened by ramparts, palisadoes, and a deep broad ditch, and by a strong redoubt on the summit of an eminence towards the SE commanding the river. The lines have been still further improved and strengthened by the erection of Fort Pitt on an eminence overlooking the town, and commanding the upper part of the river. Since the conclusion of the war, this fort has been used as an hospital. The dock-yard, barracks, and other military and naval establishments at Brompton, are thus entirely separated from the town of C., being enclosed by the line of fortifications preventing the possibility of their union with the town. The houses at Brompton within the lines are for the most part inhabited by persons connected with the barracks and dock-yard.- Connected in some degree with the defences of the town are the barracks, of which there are, first, the Lower or Marine barracks, -a large and uniform range of building enclosing a spacious quadrangle; second, the Upper or Brompton barracks, also neatly built, extensive, and commodious; third, the New or Artillery barracks, built in 1804, forming three sides of a quadrangle, and containing suitable apartments for the officers, and accommodation for 1,200 men. The open side of this quadrangle commands a charming view of the Medway, with the Thames in the distance. The garrison usually consists of five companies of soldiers and a battalion of artillery. A school for engineers was established here in 1812, in which young officers and recruits of the engineer service are trained to a practical knowledge of their duties. There is an establishment for convicts on the river, consisting of four ships, one being appropriated for juvenile offenders, and another used as an hospital. The artillery hospital, erected in 1809, contains wards for 100 patients, with offices for the medical establishment; and Melville hospital, intended for the use of the whole naval department, has been recently erected at a short distance from C., at an expense of £70,000. It forms a beautiful range of building, and has apartments for 340 patients.

CHATHAM, a county in the central part of the state of N Carolina, U. S., watered by the Haw and the Deep river, which unite to form Cape Fear river. The surface is rolling. Area 858 sq. m. The soil is clayey and fertile, producing corn, wheat, oats, cotton, and tobacco. Pop. in 1841, 16,242, of whom 5,316 were slaves. The cap. is Pittboro'.—Also a county in the SE part of Georgia, on Savannah river. The surface is level, and little elevated above tidewater. Area 405 sq. m. The chief productions are rice, cotton, and sugar. Pop. in 1841, 18,801, of whom 11,331 were slaves. The cap. is Savannah.- Also a township in Barnstable co., in the state of Massachusetts, on the SE point of Cape Cod. Its inhabitants are chiefly employed in the fisheries and coasting - trade. Pop. 2,334.—Also a township in Middlesex co., in the state of Connecticut, watered by Salmon river and Pine brook. Pop. 3,413.- CHA Also a township in Colombia co., in the state of New York, on Kinderhook creek, 16 m. NE of Hudson. Pop. 762.—Also a township in Medina co., in Ohio. Pop. 555.—Also a township in Morris co., New Jersey, 56 m. NNE of Trenton, on Passaic river. Pop. 2,138.—Also a township in Carroll co., in New Hampshire, on the E side of the White mountains. Pop. 523.—Also a township in Tioga co., in Pennsylvania. Pop. 689.—Also a village in Chester co., Pennsylvania, 68 m. ESE of Harrisburg.—Also a village in Sangamon co., Illinois, 10 m. NNW of Springfield.

CHATHAM, the county town of Kent co., in Upper Canada, situated at the entrance of Macgregor's creek into the river Thames, on the road from London to Amherstburg, 66 m. from London, and 50 m. from Detroit. Pop. 1,500. Steam-boats sail betwixt this place and Detroit. In 1844 the total value of its exports, consisting chiefly of wheat, flour, staves, furs, and skins, was £15,450.—The township of C. contains 17,119 acres. Pop. 799.-Also a parish and v. in New Brunswick, on the S side of the Miramichi river, 10 m. from its mouth, and 4 m. below Newcastle. Many timber - ships load here. Pop. 4,000.—Also a township in the co. of Ottawa, in Lower Canada, between the Ottawa river and the township of Wentworth. It is 9 m. in breadth, and 12 m. in length; and is watered by West and North rivers. Pop. in 1826, 1,073. CHATHAM BAY, PUNJO BAY, or JUAN-PONCEDE-LEON BAY, a bay on the SW coast of East Florida, in the gulf of Mexico, between Cape Romano, in N lat. 25° 50', and Sable point, the southernmost point of Florida. The distance between the two points is 19 leagues. The ground is generally clean, but the depth of water is small, and the coast lined with shoals.

CHATHAM (CAPE), a smooth steep rocky island of considerable elevation, on the S coast of Australia, in S lat. 35° 2½, E long. 116° 29'. It lies about 1 m. from a cliffy projection on the mainland. Vancouver laid down this point as a cape; but Flinders found it to be an island. Commander Stokes places it in 0° 29' 30" E of the meridian of Swan river.

CHATHAM HARBOUR, a small inlet on the SE part of the peninsula of Cape Cod, on the coast of Massachusetts, U. S., in N lat. 41° 40′ 16", W long. 69° 57′ 12". It has about 20 ft. of water at low tide; but the entrance is obstructed by sand bars. - Also an inlet among the group of small islands off the SW coast of West Falkland island. It is intersected by the meridian of 61° W.

CHATHAM ISLAND, a small island, about 2 m. long, and a | m. broad, situated in a bay of the larger Andaman island, in the bay of Bengal. A British settlement, which had been established at Port Cornwallis, was removed hither in 1793, but afterwards abandoned.
CHATHAM ISLAND. See OTDIA.

CHATHAM ISLAND, an island on the W coast of Patagonia, in S lat. 50° 35', W long. 74° 20'. It is separated from the mainland by Peel inlet, and from the N end of Hanover island, on the SW, by a continuation of the Sarmiento and San - Estevan channels. Its NW coast has numerous bights and coves fronted by islets, but affords no convenient or secure shelter .- King.

CHATHAM ISLANDS, a group in the South Pacific, discovered by Lieut. Broughton in 1791. The largest island is intersected by the parallel of 43° 45' S, and the meridian of 176° 40' W. The group consists of three islands: a large one called Warekausi by the natives, and C. island by its first discoverer; a smaller one called Rangihaute or Pitt island, about 12 m. long, and 8 m. broad, divided by A REAL BARRANCE CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR CONTRACTOR AND A CONTRACTOR AND A CONTRACTOR AND A CONTRACTOR AND A CONTRACTOR AND A CONTRACTOR AND A CONTRACTOR AND A CONTRACTOR AND A CONTRACTOR AND A CONTRACTOR AND A CON a strait of from 7 to 15 m. in breadth from the SE side of C. island; and a third, called Rangatira, or South - East island, immediately adjoining Rangihaute on the SE.—The larger island has the form of an indented square. At its NW extremity its breadth does not exceed 4 or 5 m.; that of its SE and S part is about 15 m. Dr. Dieffenbach—who has given a full description of this island in the Geog. Journal, vol. xi.—estimates the area at 477 sq. nautical m., or 305,280 acres. Of this area, however, above a sixth part is composed of lakes and lagunes. The land towards the coast seems low; projecting rocky points covered with wood interrupt the shore at unequal distances; and ranges of hills, many of them destitute of trees, and overspread with heath, rise in the interior, but none exceed 800 ft. in alt. The rocks are chiefly volcanic, and in geological formation the island may be regarded as belonging to New Zealand. A great portion of the centre of the island is occupied by a lake 25 m. long, and 6 or 7 m. broad, which approaches, on its E side, to within 100 yards of the sea; but its water is only slightly brackish. The climate is mild and agreeable; the sky generally cloudless. The flora is similar to that of New Zealand, but inferior. Potatoes, turnips, cabbages, the esculent arum, and tobacco, thrive well. Vast flocks of ducks, snipes, plovers, curlews, and redbills inhabit the lakes; and crawfish, lobsters, and cockles abound along the shores. The woods near the coast are free of undergrowth; and consist of various trees exhibiting luxuriant vegetation. The native inhabitants of this island are of middling size, some of them stout, well made, and muscular; they are of a dark brown colour, their hair and beard black; their features plain, and their teeth in general bad. Their numbers were about 1,200 a few years ago; but they have been nearly extirpated by the New Zealanders, who, to the number of 800, have settled on this island.—Waitangi bay, in 43° 58' S, and 176° 38' W, has a general depth of from 7 to 12 fath. It is much frequented by whaling vessels. The tide is irregular here, coming from the S, and generally recurring only once in 24 hours.
CHATHAM (PORT), a harbour on the E side of Vancouver's island, in N lat. 50° 20'.
CHATHAM STRAIT, a narrow channel on the NW coast of North America, which divides King George the Third's archipelago from Admiralty island.

CHATILLON, a stream of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, flowing into the Vezouze, opposite Cirey, after a course of 10 m.—Also a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Drôme, arrond. of Die. The cant. comprises 10 com., with a pop. in 1831 of 6,840. The com., 6 m. SE of Die, had a pop. of 1,195 in 1841.—Also a commune and village in the dep. of Rhône, cant. of Bois-d'Oingt, on the Azergue, 9 m. SSW of Villefranche. Pop. 841.— Also a town of the Sardinian states, in the prov. of Aosta, on the l. bank of the Dora-Baltea, 14 m. E of Aosta. Pop. 1,800. It is surrounded with magnificent scenery.
CHATILLON - SOUS - BAGNEUX, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Seine, arrond. and 2 m. N of Sceaux. Pop. 1,107.

CHATILLON-EN-BAZOIS, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Nièvre, arrond. of Chateau - Chinon. - The cant., comprising 16 com., had a pop. in 1831 of 10,713.—The com., 15 m. W of Chateau-Chinon, on the Aron, had a pop. of 1,012 in 1841.
CHATILLON-SUR-COLMONT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Goron, 6 m. WNW of Mayenne. Pop. 2,504.

CHATILLON - LES - DOMBES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ain, arrond. | in Europe, in the sanjak of Monastir, on a small CHA of Trevoux.—The cant., comprising 16 com., had a pop. of 12,827 in 1831.—The com. and town, 15 m. NNE of Trevoux, on the Chalaronne, had a pop. in 1841 of 3,202.
CHATILLON - EN - DUNOIS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Eure-et-Loire, cant. of Cloyes. Pop. 1,222.

CHATILLON - SUR - INDRE, or INDREMONT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Indre, arrond. of Chateauroux. The cant., comprising 10 com., had a pop. of 10,514 in 1831. The com. and town, on the l. bank of the Indre, 30 m. WNW of Chateauroux, had a pop. of 3,542 in 1841.

CHATILLON - SUR - LOING, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Loiret, arrond. of Montargis.-The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,570.—The com. and town, on the l. bank of the Loing, 15 m. SSE of Montargis, had a pop. of 2,067 in 1841. Admiral Coligny was born in the family chateau at this town in 1516.

CHATILLON-SUR-LOIRE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Loiret, arrond. of Gien.—The cant. comprising 6 com., had a pop. of 7,712 in 1331.—The com. and town, on the l. bank of the Loire, 10 m. SE of Gien, had a pop. of 2,208 in 1841. Marble and building-stone are quarried in the vicinity.

CHATILLON - SUR - MARNE, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Marne, arrond. of Reims.—The cant., comprising 19 com., had a pop. of 7,337 in 1831.—The com. and v., on the r. bank of the Marne, 18 m. SW of Reims, had a pop. of 248 in 1841.

CHATILLON-DE-MICHAILLE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ain, arrond. of Nantua.—The cant., comprising 16 com., had a pop. of 9,793 in 1831.—The com., 10 m. E of Nantua, on the Valserine, had a pop. of 1,453 in 1841.
CHATILLON - SUR - SAONE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Vosges, cant. of Lamarche, at the confluence of the Amance with the Saone. Pop. 698.

CHATILLON-SUR-SEINE, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Cote-d'Or.—The arrond has an area of 186,792 hectares; and comprises the 6 cant. of Aignay-le- Duc, Baigneux - les - Juifs, C., Laignes, Montignysur-Aube, and Recey-sur-Ource. - The cant. comprises 28 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,654.—The com. and town, situated on the Seine, 30 m. NNE of Semur-en-Auxois, and 45 m. NW of Dijon, had a pop. of 2,418 in 1789; and 4,779 in 1841. It is the seat of a communal college, and a tribunal of commerce; and has a public library of 7,500 vols. Woollen cloth, paper, and leather, are manufactured here; and lithographic stones are quarried in the vicinity. There is a fine chateau here, in which a congress of representatives of the allied powers was held in February 1814, whereat it was determined to offer Napoleon the throne of France on certain conditions which he rejected.

CHATILLON - SUR - SE'VRE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Deux- Sevres, arrond. of Bressuire.—The cant. comprises 13 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,764.—The town, 15 m. WNW of Bressuire, had a pop. of 935 in 1841. It is an ancient place, and bore the name of MAULE'ON until 1737.
CHATILLON - EN - VENDELAIS, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, com. and 8 m. N of Vitré, on the lake of Cantache. Pop. 1,542.
CHATISTA, or NASLIDTSCH, a town of Turkey affluent of the Nazilitza, 25 m. S by E of Cailari, on the slope of Mount Bourenos.
CHATLEY, a hamlet in the p. of Great Leighs, Essex. Area 1,810 acres. Pop. 564.
CHATNA, a town of Hindostan, in Bengal, in the Jungle-Mahals, 108 m. from Calcutta.
CHATONAY, a com. and town of France, in the dep. of Isère, cant. of Saint-Jean-de-Bournay. Pop. 3,056.
CHATOU, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Saint-Germainen-Laye, on the r. bank of the Seine, 6 m. N of Versailles. Pop. 1,100.

CHATRE (LA), an arrondissement, canton, and commune of France, in the dep. of Indre. - The arrond. has an area of 135,886 hectares; and comprises 5 cant.: Aigurande, La C., Eguzon, Neuvi- Saint-Sepulchre, and Sainte-Severe. Pop. in 1831, 52,497; in 1841, 54,591.—The cant. comprises 19 com. Pop. in 1831, 16,679.—The com. and town are situated on the Indre, 21 m. SE of Chateauroux. It is a well-built town; and has considerable manufactures of woollen cloth and leather, and a large commerce in cattle and chestnuts. Pop. in 1789, 3,536; in 1841, 4,635.
CHATRE - LANGLIN (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Indre, cant. of Saint-Benoistdu-Sault, 20 m. SE of Le Blanc. Pop. 1,372.
CHATRESSAC, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of La Tremblade, on an arm of the Seudré, 6 m. from the sea. Pop. 135.
CHATRAKAL. See CHITTELDRUG.
CHATSU, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 24 m. SSE of Ajmere. It has lost its former consequence.

CHATTAHOOCHEE, a river of the United States, which rises in the Appalachian mountains, and, flowing SSW and S, forms throughout a considerable distance the boundary between the states of Georgia and Alabama. It joins the Flint river at the point where that river enters Florida, and with it constitutes the Apalichicola; and is navigable for steamboats to Columbus, 300 m. above the confluence of the two rivers, and at the lower end of a series of falls or rapids over which the C. descends 111 ft. in a distance of 4 m. The C. just below the falls is 354 ft. wide, but soon widens to 250 yards. The name of this river signifies in the Indian language 'flowered stones,' and is said to have been conferred on it on account of the quantity of stones of various colours which strew the bottom of the river.-Also a town in Gadsden co., in the state of Florida, on the E side of the Apalichicola, 45 m. W of Tallahassee.

CHATTAR, a walled town of some size, with considerable cultivation in its vicinity, in Kutch-Gandava, 10 m. NNE of Shahpur, and 4 m. S of Sehada. It was formerly the stronghold of the Jekrani tribe of Beluchis.

CHATTE (CAPE), a round projecting sugarloafshaped point on the S side of the gulf of St. Lawrence, in N. lat. 49° 6', W long. 66° 45'. About 234 m. E of the cape, a river of the same name enters a small sandy bay.-The St. Anne mountains attain an alt. of 3,970 ft., about 14 m. to the N of the cape. The shore along this line of coast is of highly inclined slate and greywacke rocks, and the country behind quite desert and impracticable.
CHATTERGHUR, a town of India, in the prov. of Agra, 26 m. E by S of Gwalior.
CHATTERIS, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 10 m. NW of Ely. Area 15,090 acres. Pop. 4,813.
CHATTERLEY, a township in the p. of Wolstanton in Staffordshire. Pop. 374.

CHATTERPUR, a town of Hindostan. in the prov. CHA and most flourishing town in Bundlekund." There are several extensive paper manufactories here, and a considerable amount of coarse cutlery is annually produced within the city. The surrounding scenery is very romantic.
CHATTISHAM, a parish in Suffolk, 5 m. ESE of Hadleigh. Area 2,480 acres. Pop. 215.
CHATTON, a parish in Northumberland, 44 m. E. of Wooler, intersected by the Till river. Area 16,930 acres. Pop. 1,725.
CHATTOOGA, a county in the NW part of Georgia. Area 300 sq. m. Pop. 3,438. The cap. is Summerville.
CHATTRA, a town of Northern India, in Nepal, 82 m. N by W of Purnea, in N lat. 26° 53'.
CHATTUE, a town of Bengal, in the prov. of Silhet, on the l. bank of the Surmah, 12 m. NW of Silhet.
CHATUZENGE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Drôme, cant. of Bourg-du- Peage. Pop. 1,737.
CHAUCALLA, a town of Peru, on the l. bank of the Rio Grande, 65 m. from its mouth, in Slat. 15° 12'.
CHAUCHE', a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Saint-Fulgent. Pop. 1,499.
CHAUDEAU (LE), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Saint-Loup. Pop. 183.
CHAUDEFONDS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Chalonnes, on the Layen. Pop. 1,286.

CHAUDESAIGUES, a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Cantal, arrond. of Saint-Fleur.—The cant., comprising 13 com., had a pop. of 9,074 in 1831.—The com. and v., 12 m. SSW of Saint-Fleur, had a pop. of 2,476 in 1841. The v., situated in a pleasant valley surrounded by high mountains, has some celebrity on account of its hot mineral waters. Of these the Par has a temp. of 80° C. or 170° F. By means of ingeniously contrived conduits, the inhabitants of the v. have conducted this water into their houses so as to give them warmth during winter; in summer they turn it away towards the river. M. Berthier has calculated that, as a heating agent, the water of the Par is equivalent to the wood which could be furnished by 540 hectares, = 1,334 acres of oak-forest. The water issues over a sulphuret of iron, and is analogous to that of Plombières.
CHAUDEYRAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Lozere, cant. of Chateauneuf-de-Randon. Pop. 1,071.
CHAUDFONTAINE, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. and 4 m. SE of Liege, on the r. bank of the Vesdre. Pop. 973. There are mineral alkaline springs and baths here.

CHAUDIE'RE, a river of Lower Canada, in the co. of Beauce and of Dorchester. It has its source near the boundary line between Canada and the United States, and the sources of the Connecticut, at an alt. of 1,610 ft., in some small streams which rise between Gipp's peak [alt. 3,060 ft.], and Mount Gosford [alt. 3,665 ft.]. It flows S into Lake Megantic; and after a N and NW course of 120 m., falls into the St. Lawrence, 6 m. above Quebec. Its breadth varies from 400 to 600 yards, and the stream is frequently divided by islands, some of them containing many acres, and covered with wood. The banks are in general high, rocky, and steep, and clothed with wood; the bed is much contracted by rocks, which, jutting out on each side, occasion violent rapids, and frequently give rise to considerable falls, of which the most remarkable are those called the Chaudière falls, about 4 m. above its influx into the St. Lawand 135 m. WSW of Allahabad. It is "the cleanest | rence. The river is seen at a distance, emerging ~<del>~</del> from a thick wood, and gradually expanding, from an almost imperceptible stream, till it nears the cataract, where salient points of rock again contract it to a breadth of about 360 ft. [400 ft. Arfwedson; 240 yds., Macgregor; "scarcely more than" 130 yds., Bouchette; "not more than 400 ft.," Silliman]. Here the disordered masses of rock, which appear to have been rent from their bed by some violent convulsion of nature, break the course of the waters, and precipitate them from a height of 100 ft. [135 ft. Arfivedson; more than 100 ft. Macgregor; about 130 ft. Bouchette and Silliman.] into an immense chasm below. In some parts large sheets of water roll over the precipice, and fall unbroken to the bottom; while in other places the water dashes from one fragment of the rock to another, with wild impetuosity, bellowing and foaming in every hollow and cavity that obstruct its progress—and then in a single undivided mass, rushing down with the rapidity of lightning into the boiling, restless whirlpool beneath-whence its French name signifying 'the Pot'—where it rages with inconceivable fury, till it hurries away and loses itself in the waters of the St. Lawrence. The forest on either side the river consists of firs, pines, birch, oak, ash, and a variety of other trees and shrubs, intermingled in a wild and romantic manner. Their dark green foliage, joined with the brown and sombre tint of the rocky fragments over which the water precipitates itself, form a striking and pleasing contrast to the snowy whiteness of the foaming surge, and the columns of sparkling spray which rise in clouds and mingle with the air. The river itself is of no utility as a water-conveyance; it is scarcely navigable even for canoes.

CHAUDIERES (LAC DES), a lake of North America, in Canada, formed by the river Ottawa, and computed to be 18 m. in length, and from 2 to 4 m. in breadth. Though it is called a lake, a strong current runs downwards, and it is strictly a mere expansion of the river, commencing 6 m. below the falls Des Chats, and terminating in rapids, succeeded by falls, called the Great and the Little Chaudière, which are together 60 ft. high, and 212 ft. broad. The Ottawa has been rendered navigable from the river St. Lawrence up to these falls. The two provinces are united by a series of bridges passing from island to island on the river at the falls. One bridge or arch in the series has a span of 200 ft. Two arches have each 70 ft. span; and 8 others are each of 60 ft. span.
CHAUDRON, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Montrevault, 6 m. N. of Beaupreau. Pop. 1,581.

CHAUFFAILLES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Saone-et-Loire, arrond. of Charolles.—The cant. comprises 9 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,801.—The com. and town are 14 m. S of Botoret. Pop. 3,570.
CHAUKNA, or CHAKIM, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Aurungabad, 20 m. N of Punah, on an affluent of the Bima.
CHAULGNES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Nievre, cant. of La Charité. Pop. 1,378.
CHAULNES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Somme, arrond. of Peronne. -The cant. comprises 23 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,306.—The com. and town are 10 m. SW of Peronne. Pop. 1,258.
CHAUMAI (CAPE), a promontory on the coast of Cochin China, 30 m. NW of Cape Turon, in N lat. 16° 22'.
CHAUME (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Vendee, com. of La Sables. Pop. 1,000.

CHAUMERGY, a canton, commune, and village CHA of France, in the dep. of Jura, arrond. of Dole.—The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,084.—The com., 15 m. S. of Dole, on the Brene, had a pop. of 436 in 1841.
CHAUMES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Tournon, on the Yares river, 12 m. NE of Malun. Pop. 1,705.

CHAUMONT, an arrondissement, canton, and commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne.- The arrond, has an area of 249,700 hectares, and comprises the 10 cant. of Andelot, Arc-en-Barrois, Saint-Blin, Bourmont, Chateau-Vilain, Chaumonten-Bassigny, Clefmont, Juzennecourt, Nogent-le- Roi, and Vignory. Pop. in 1831, 85,065; in 1841, 87,394.—The cant. comprises 22 com. Pop. in 1831, 12,021.—The com. and town are on the l. bank of the Marne, and near the confluence of the Suize, 139 m. ESE of Paris, at an alt. of 1,063 ft. above sea-level. It is a well built but somewhat dull-looking town. Its fortifications were repaired in 1821. Pop. in 1789, 5,902; in 1841, 6,347. It has a communal college, a normal school, and a public library of 35,000 vols. Its trade is brisk; its druggets, hosiery, and cutlery are in repute, and it has a large trade in iron.—On the 1st of March 1814, England, Austria, Prussia, and Russia concluded here a treaty of alliance, against France.
CHAUMONT, a town of the Sardinian states, in Savoy, in the prov. of Carouge, 10 m. SW of Saint- Julien. Pop. 1,000.
CHAUMONT, a village in Ligne township, in Jefferson co., in the state of New York, 178 m. NW of Albany, at the E end of Lake Ontario, on a river of the same name. Pop. 300.
CHAUMONT-SUR-LOIRE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Montrichard, on the l. bank of the Loire. Pop. 985.

CHAUMONT-PORCIEN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, arrond. of Rethel.—The cant., comprising 20 com., had a pop. of 10,067 in 1831.—The com. and town, 12 m. NNW of Rethel, on the Aisne, had a pop. of 1,091 in 1841.
CHAUMONT-SUR-THARONNE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Loir-et-Cher, cant. of La Motte-Beuvron. Pop. 1,197.

CHAUMONT-EN-VEXIN, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Oise, arrond. of Beauvais.—The cant., comprising 36 com., had a pop. of 13,266 in 1831.—The com. and town, 18 m. SSW of Beauvais on the Troène, had a pop. of 1,126 in 1841.
CHAUNAI, a commune of France, in the dep. of Vienne, cant. of Couhé, on the Bouleur. Pop. 2,163.
CHAUNCHRA, or CHANKRA, a town of Hindostan, in Malwah, 42 m. W of Seronge.
CHAUNI, a small village of Upper India, belonging to the raja of Chicari, between Chicari and Atgar, in the Bundlecund territory. Barley, grain, and teesa, are extensively cultivated in the environs.

CHAUNY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aisne, arrond. of Laon.—The cant. comprises 19 com. Pop. in 1831, 18,025.—The com. and town are on the Oise, at the commencement of the canal of Saint-Quentin, 65 m. NE of Paris, at an alt. of 64 metres = 209 ft. above sealevel. Pop. 5,154 in 1841. Linen, hosiery, leather, and chemical wares are manufactured here; and the place forms an entrepot for coal.
CHAURIAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Vertaizon. Pop. 1,461.
CHAUSSAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Rhone, cant. of Mornaut. Pop. 1,310.
CHAUSSE'E (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Marne, cant. of Vitry-le-Francais. Pop. 838.

CHAUSSEY, a small group of islets and rocks belonging to France, in the dep. of Manche, opposite Granville, and occupying a space of nearly 16 sq. m., or about 8 m. in length by 11 to 21 m. in width. The largest is about 1 m. in length, and a 1 m. in width. It is 19 m. from La Roque in Jersey; 11 m. from Maitre-Ile-des-Minquiers, and 9 m. from Granville. There are several small harbours within and among these rocks. There is a lighthouse on the S point of the larger island, in N lat. 48° 52′ 13", E long. 1° 49′ 13".—These islets furnish excellent granite for building. Granville and St. Maloes are built of this stone.

CHAUSSON, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Jura, arrond. of Dole.—The cant. comprises 20 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,382.—The com. and town, near the l. bank of the Doubs, 10 m. SSW of Dole, had a pop. of 1,328 in 1841.
CHAUSSY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Magny, 10 m. NNW of Mantes. Pop. 969.

CHAUTAUQUE, the extreme W county of the state of New York, U. S., extending 30 m. along Lake Erie, on which it has several harbours. Area 1,007 sq. m. Pop. in 1830, 34,057; in 1840, 47,975. Its surface is hilly. A ridge of from 1,000 to 1,400 ft. above tide-water, and from 5 to 8 m. from the lake, divides the waters flowing into Lake Erie from those which flow into the gulf of Mexico.—The cap., of the same name, is 336 m. W by S of Albany. Pop. 2,980. -In the central part of the county lies a beautiful sheet of water, called C. lake, 18 m. long, and from 1 to 3 m. broad, at an alt. of 726 ft. above Lake Erie, and 1,291 ft. above sea-level. It is navigable for steam-boats from Maryville to its lower end; and thence by boats to Connewango creek, a tributary of Alleghany river, whence rafts can proceed to the gulf of Mexico.
CHAUTAY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Cher, cant. of La Guerche. Pop. 374.
CHAUVANCY-LE-CHATEAU, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. and 3 m. W of Montmedy, on the r. bank of the Chiers. Pop. 592.
CHAUVANCY-SAINT-HUBERT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, com. and 4 m. W of Montmedy. Pop. 577.
CHAUVE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Saint-Pere-en-Retz, 10 m. S of Paimbœuf. Pop. 1,565.

CHAUVIGNY, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Vienne, arrond. of Montrillon. -The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. 7,688 in 1831. -The com. and town, 18 m. NW of Montrillon, on the r. bank of the Vienne, had a pop. of 1,768 in 1841. Extensive tanneries exist here; and druggets and serges are manufactured, and a considerable quantity of red wine is grown.

CHAUX-DE-FOND (LA), a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 8 m. NW of Neufchatel, in a bleak and rugged valley of the Jura, at an alt. of 3,280 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 8,481. It is a place of active industry, and particularly celebrated for its manufacture of watches. The number of gold and silver watches made here in 1836 was 108,295. Lace also is extensively made.
CHAUX-DE-MILIEU (LA), a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 12 m. W of Neufchatel. Pop. 450.
CHAUX-NEUVE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. of Mouthe. Pop. 709.
CHAVAGNES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Thouarcé. Pop. 1,171.
CHAVANAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire, cant. of Belussin, 18 m. E of Saint-Etienne. Pop. 1,772. CHA

CHAVANGES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Aube, arrond. of Arcis-sur- Aube.—The cant., comprising 14 com., had a pop. of 4,220 in 1831.—The com. and town are 20 m. E of Arcis-sur-Aube. Pop. 1,080. CHAVANNE-SUR-L'ETANG, [GERM. Kleinschaffnatt, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. of Fontaine. Pop. 551.
CHAVANNES-EN-PAILLERS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Vendée, cant. of Saint-Fulgent. Pop. 2,008.
CHAVANNES-SUR-REYSSOUSE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Pont-de-Vaux. Pop. 1,191.
CHAVANNES-SUR-SURAN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Treffort, on the r. bank of the Suran. Pop. 1,259.
CHAVANON, or Chavanoux, a river of France, an affluent of the Dordogne, which it joins on the r. bank a little above Bort, after a course of 30 m.
